chapters.title: Combustion, Fuels and Flame
topics: A chemical process in which a material reacts with oxygen present in the air to generate heat is called combustion
8.1 What is needed for  the process of  combustion?

Lab Activity: Aim:  To prove that oxygen helps in burning
Material required:  Test tube, test tube holder, spirit lamp, match box, incence stick (agarbatti), potassium permanganate crystals.

topics: The substances which have very low ignition temperature and easily catch fire are called Inflammable Substances
The type of combustion in which material suddenly  burns into flames without the application of any external agent is called spontaneous combustion
Such combustion is known as rapid combustion
Explosions can also take place if pressure is applied on the crackers.
Calorific value  of a fuel is the amount of heat energy produced on complete combustion of 1 kg of that fuel.

chapters.title: Chemical Equations
topics: Step 1 : Write the equation with the correct chemical formulae for each reactant and product.
Step 2 : After writing the molecular formulae of the substances the equation is to be balanced.
Step 3 : Sometimes there is a possibility that the coefficients of all the substances getting divided with a suitable number.
Step 4:  V erify the equation for the balancing of atoms on both sides of the equations.
Eg-1:  Combustion of propane (C3H8) in presence of oxygen
Step 1:  Write the unbalanced equation using correct chemical formulae for all substances.
Step 2:  Compare number of atoms of each element on both sides. Find the coefficients to balance the equation.
Step 3:  Make sure the coefficients are reduced to their smallest whole-number values.
Step 4: Check the answer. Count the numbers and kinds of atoms on both sides of the equation to make sure they are the same.
Eg-2: Iron oxide reacts with Aluminum to form iron and Aluminum trioxide.
Step 1: Write the equation using the correct chemical symbols and formulae for all the reactants and products.
Step 2: Find the suitable coefficient for the reactants and products, to equate the number of atoms of each element on both sides.
